rationale: The citing paper uses Goldstein et al. (2016) in its framing-intervention theory section to support a specific claim: equivalent retirement resources can produce different judgments and saving intentions depending on presentation format (annuity-like monthly amounts vs lump sums). This citation is not just a background list entry; it helps justify why the authors include payment-framing manipulations when studying Social Security claiming intentions. The citing paper does not dispute or re-estimate Goldstein et al.'s effects, but leverages that prior mechanism as motivation in a new decision context.
relation_description: The citing paper applies the cited paper's format-framing insight from retirement wealth evaluations to Social Security claiming-age interventions. It uses the prior evidence to motivate testing whether benefit presentation can shift claiming intentions.
